imageCaption: The Gomez family's yellow-doored house in Willemstad, Curaçao, morning light catching the edges of walls that are still deciding which time period they belong to.
text:
The house woke them up at 4:47 AM by existing very insistently.

Not in a threatening way. Just with the kind of presence that things have when they've finally stopped being theoretical and become actual. The walls had a solidity they hadn't quite committed to the previous evening. The floor beneath Aaliyah's feet, when she stumbled toward what she was almost certain was a kitchen, didn't waver between marble and wood. It had made a decision. The marble had won.

She found her father already awake, standing in front of the pegboard, which he'd finally mounted on the kitchen wall sometime after midnight when everyone was supposedly sleeping. The license plates caught the pre-dawn light coming through the window—and the window, notably, was no longer confused about which ocean it was supposed to show. It had settled on the present-day Atlantic, current tense, Caribbean blue.

"Morning," Anthony said without turning around. "The house tried to teach me where the coffee is about an hour ago. I'm not 100% sure I understand the communication method, but there's definitely coffee. Very insistent coffee. The kitchen cabinet opened seventeen times until I paid attention."

"Seventeen," Aaliyah repeated. "The house is a 17-times communicator. Good to know."

"Apparently." Anthony was still studying the pegboard. He'd rearranged it at some point—it wasn't a random collection anymore. It followed a path. Lisbon to Madrid to Buenos Aires to Tokyo to São Paulo, a curving line of everywhere they'd been, and then at the end, the Curaçao plate glowed softly in the early light. "Your mother's going to wake up at 6:30. Your brother will be awake at 5:15 because he always is and for some reason his internal clock doesn't care about being on vacation. Sully will sleep until someone eats breakfast."

"How do you know that?" Aaliyah asked, though she already knew he was probably right. Anthony had been reading his family's patterns like maps for twenty-three years.

"Because that's what they've been doing for seventy-four days," Anthony said. "Even when we were in glitch zones, even when time was skipping sideways, your mother still needed 6:30 AM to face the world. Elijah still woke up early like he had a very important meeting with the sun. Sully still believed breakfast was the most important event in any timeline."

Aaliyah settled into one of the kitchen chairs—they were coral-colored, she noticed, deliberately cheerful—and tried to process what it meant to be standing in a house made of yellow doors and settled decisions.

"I keep waiting for something to be wrong," she admitted. "For ARIA-7 to show up on the fridge telling us our schedule is suboptimal. For something to glitch. For reality to remember we broke it and come collect payment."

Anthony turned from the pegboard. His expression was the one he used when explaining things that other people might find complicated but which he understood with a kind of bone-deep certainty.

"The Cascade doesn't punish people for breaking it," he said. "It stabilizes around emotional resonance patterns. We didn't break it by planning too much or following algorithm too closely. Those things broke it because they were cold. They didn't have heart in them. But you know what does have heart? A family that spent seventy-four days flying toward nowhere in particular and accidentally healing the entire timeline just by loving each other while doing it."

"So we're just... allowed to be here," Aaliyah said slowly. "The house is allowed to exist here. Time is allowed to move forward normally."

"I think," Anthony said, "the universe is very relieved we finally stopped and decided to mean something."

The coffee cabinet had closed itself by 5:00 AM, apparently satisfied that Anthony understood its point. Elijah had woken up at 5:13, three minutes earlier than Anthony's prediction, and had wandered into the kitchen to announce that the bathroom had seventeen different configurations and he was going to figure out which one was the "correct" one through a scientific process involving jumping in all of them.

"Don't," Luisana said, appearing in the doorway at exactly 6:30 AM, which meant Anthony's internal family clock was maintained to clock-precision accuracy. "Don't test the house's boundaries yet. It's still settling. You'll give it ideas."

She was holding her journal—the usual one, coffee-stained and filled with shorthand observations that only she could fully decipher. But she'd added something new to the cover. A small, pressed morpho butterfly, carefully preserved under clear tape.

"Where did you get that?" Anthony asked, but his voice had already softened with understanding.

"Found it on the doorstep this morning," Luisana said. "Along with this." She pulled out a small note, handwritten in a script that looked like it had been written by someone who had moved a lot: "Your yellow door is finally home. Build something beautiful."

No signature. But Aaliyah understood anyway. Isla wasn't part of the timeline. But she was still part of the family's story—the part that existed in the threshold, the part that helped people make it to where they needed to be.

"It's not a glitch," Elijah said firmly, looking at the butterfly like it was a fact he needed to establish. "It's a gift. Abuela Ida is saying hi. Abuela Ida is saying welcome home."

Sully, having finally decided that breakfast was not going to happen on its own, wandered into the kitchen and immediately claimed ownership of the coral chair next to Aaliyah. He rested his chin on her knee with the confidence of a dog who understood that he'd been part of healing an entire timeline and expected to be moderately commemorated for it.

"So what do we do now?" Aaliyah asked. "The story doesn't usually have an ending like this. The family isn't supposed to stay. The journey is supposed to keep going."

"Maybe," Luisana said, opening her journal to a blank page, "our story is just learning to tell itself in a new way. Maybe the journey doesn't end. Maybe it just learns to have a home to return to."

She started writing. Not descriptions this time. Questions: What does it mean to choose? What does a family build when it stops running? What color is the door to the rest of your life?

And in the yellow morning light of a house that had spent seventy-four days figuring out how to be real, Aaliyah realized that the story wasn't ending at all.

It was just beginning to become the one that mattered.
